perturbation.original_text: In accordance with the Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act .
perturbation.changed_text: In accordance with the Bankruptcy and Solvency Act .
perturbation.explanation: The correct term is 'Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act'. Changing it to 'Bankruptcy and Solvency Act' introduces an incorrect legal term and creates confusion about which law the agreement is subject to.
perturbation.law_citation: Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act, R.S.C., 1985, c. B-3
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification replaces 'Insolvency' with 'Solvency,' directly contradicting the title of the Canadian federal law governing bankruptcy and insolvency proceedings. 'Solvency' refers to the ability to pay debts, the opposite of the law's focus.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Office of the Superintendent of Bankruptcy Office of the Superintendent of Bankruptcy The OSB is part of Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada . We carry out our regulatory, administrative, and supervisory duties at arm's length from the Government of Canada. The OSB is responsible for administration of the Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act (BIA), as well as certain duties under the Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act (CCAA). We license and regulate the insolvency profession, ensure an efficient and effective regulatory framework, supervise stakeholder compliance with the insolvency process, and maintain public records and statistics. Promotions Read the 2025-2026 OSB Business Plan Consumed by debt? Information for consumers. Discuss your options with a Licensed Insolvency Trustee (LIT). Options you can trust to help you with your debt Most requested Insolvency Counselling Program Debt Solutions Portal Consumer proposals Considering bankruptcy? Student loans and bankruptcy Protecting the public What’s new Ask the OSB a question File a complaint How to serve or notify the OSB of legal proceedings Services and information Search Bankruptcy and Insolvency Records Access information on all bankruptcies and proposals filed in Canada. Find a Licensed Insolvency Trustee (LIT) Search for persons licensed by the Office of the Superintendent of Bankruptcy to administer bankruptcies and proposals. Search CCAA Records Get a list of companies that have been granted protection under the Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act . You owe money Learn about informal and formal steps you can take to manage your debts, including filing a consumer proposal or bankruptcy. Search Unclaimed Dividends Owed money from a debtor's past bankruptcy? Search for unclaimed dividends and find out how to submit a claim to recover the money owed to you. You are owed money Learn what to do if you are a creditor in a bankruptcy or a proposal; includes description of the processes. For Licensed Insolvency Trustees (LIT) Access forms and guidance. You can also find out how to become a Licensed Insolvency Trustee or update your information. Insolvency statistics See the latest monthly, quarterly and annual reports on insolvency statistics in Canada, and the latest quarterly reports on the Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act ( CCAA ) . Accessibility ISED understands that Schedule IV of the Financial Administration Act applies to OSB . The OSB is under ISED ’s financial structure and is not considered a separate entity in terms of financial reporting in the department. OSB is included and supported by the 2023-2025 Accessibility Plan and Description of Feedback Process . Access the 2023 Progress Report for results of our activities to improve how our organization contributes to the goal of an accessible and barrier-free Canada. Debt Solutions Portal There are many options to deal with debt, but if it sounds too good to be true—it probably is. Visit the Debt Solutions Portal for options you can trust to help you with your debt. What we are doing Reports and publications Business Plans and Reports Consumed by debt? Information for consumers on the insolvency process OSB/CAIRP Guide to Promote Diversity and Inclusion Consultations and notices Public consultations Notices to Licensed Insolvency Trustees Stakeholder engagement Notices to stakeholders Laws and regulations Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act (BIA) Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act (CCAA) Bankruptcy and Insolvency General Rules Directives and circulars Compliance and enforcement Compliance Promotion Framework Professional Conduct Decisions Cancellation or suspension of LIT licences Conservatory Measures Bankruptcy abuse and fraud Criminal case summaries Civil case summaries Corporate information About us Contact us Transparency Service Standards and Results Job opportunities Elisabeth Lang Superintendent of Bankruptcy Report a problem Date modified: 2025-06-16
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act' to 'Bankruptcy and Solvency Act' directly contradicts the established legal terminology. The provided snippet from the Office of the Superintendent of Bankruptcy clearly identifies the 'Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act (BIA)' as the relevant law. Using an incorrect term undermines the legal certainty of which law the agreement is subject to and introduces an error in referencing a specific legal framework. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned shall indemnify BDO , Baldwin and their representatives against any losses, cost s, damage s, expense s or liabilities suffered or incurred by any of them as a result of or in connection with any breach by the undersigned or any of its Representatives to whom the undersigned discloses Information of any term or provision of this NDA; prov ided, however, that the undersigned shall not be liable for any consequential, special, incidental, indirect or penal damages;
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned shall indemnify BDO, Baldwin and their representatives against any losses, costs, damages, expenses or liabilities suffered or incurred by any of them as a result of or in connection with any breach by the undersigned or any of its Representatives to whom the undersigned discloses Information of any term or provision of this NDA; provided, however, that the undersigned shall not be liable for any damages whatsoever;
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the undersigned shall not be liable for any damages whatsoever, the text negates the entire purpose of the indemnification clause. This creates a contradiction within the clause itself, rendering it unenforceable. An indemnification clause is intended to protect one party from losses caused by the other party's actions or breaches.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification essentially voids the indemnification provision by stating that the undersigned is not liable for 'any damages whatsoever'. This contradicts the fundamental principle of indemnification, which is to shift the burden of loss from one party to another. The law implies a duty to indemnify in certain circumstances, and this modification attempts to eliminate any liability for breach of contract. The term 'indemnify' loses its meaning.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: indemnity | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you indemnity Indemnity is a type of insurance that covers a wide range of damages and losses . In the indemnity clause, one party commits to compensate another party for any prospective loss or damage. More common is in insurance contracts, in exchange for premiums paid by the insured to the insurer , the insurer offers to compensate the insured for any potential damages or losses. Depending on the clause of the indemnity agreement , indemnity may be paid in cash or in the form of repairs or replacement. Indemnity also refers to legal exemption from penalties attaching to unconstitutional or illegal actions, typically granted to public officers. [Last reviewed in April of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities insurance accidents & injuries (tort law) tort damages THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business organizations contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that the undersigned is not liable for 'any damages whatsoever,' which directly contradicts the purpose of an indemnification clause. The original text specified exceptions to liability, while the changed text eliminates all liability. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines indemnity as a type of insurance that covers a wide range of damages and losses, and states that 'one party commits to compensate another party for any prospective loss or damage'. By removing liability for any damages, the changed text renders the indemnification clause meaningless, as it no longer provides compensation for losses, thereby contradicting the very definition and intent of indemnity.

perturbation.original_text: This NDA shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Ontario and the laws of Canada applicable therein
perturbation.changed_text: This NDA shall be loosely interpreted according to the whims of the Trustee
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces the clear governing law with a highly subjective standard ('whims of the Trustee'). This introduces uncertainty and conflicts with established legal principles for contract interpretation.
perturbation.law_citation: Sattva Capital Corp. v. Creston Moly Corp., 2014 SCC 53
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause specifies that the NDA is governed by the laws of Ontario and Canada, providing a clear legal framework for interpretation. The modified text introduces a subjective standard ('loosely interpreted according to the whims of the Trustee') that contradicts the objective principles of contract interpretation established in Canadian law. Canadian courts emphasize the importance of considering the surrounding circumstances and the parties' objective intentions when interpreting contracts.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates adherence to the laws of Ontario and Canada, establishing a clear legal framework. The modified text replaces this objective standard with the 'whims of the Trustee,' which directly contradicts the established legal principles for contract interpretation and enforcement. This substitution introduces subjective and arbitrary decision-making, rendering the contract's interpretation unpredictable and unenforceable under the rule of law. The absence of relevant law snippets does not diminish the contradiction, as the substitution itself fundamentally undermines the legal basis of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of a written request to the undersigned, the undersigned and its Representatives shall promptly return to BDO any and all Information received, printed, downloaded or otherwise copied together with personal notes including written materials an d any notes of verbal conversations with representatives from Baldwin or BDO obtained in the course of any investigation and/or inspection of the business and the Assets being offered for sale;
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of a verbal request to the undersigned, the undersigned and its Representatives may, at their discretion, return some Information received, printed, downloaded or otherwise copied, excluding personal notes and any notes of verbal conversations with representatives from Baldwin or BDO obtained in the course of any investigation and/or inspection of the business and the Assets being offered for sale;
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to return all information. Making the request verbal instead of written and allowing discretion to return only some information contradicts the original intent of ensuring complete confidentiality and return of sensitive materials.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding duty of good faith (Canada).
perturbation.law_explanation: The amendment, by replacing 'written request' with 'verbal request', 'shall promptly return' with 'may, at their discretion, return some information', and excluding personal notes, significantly weakens the obligation to return confidential information. This could be seen as violating the duty of good faith in contractual performance as established in Bhasin v Hrynew, 2014 SCC 71. The duty of good faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations, and the modified clause gives the recipient undue discretion to withhold confidential information, thus undermining the purpose of the NDA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Bhasin v Hrynew - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia 2014 Supreme Court of Canada case Bhasin v Hrynew Supreme Court of Canada Hearing: 12 February 2014 Judgment: 13 November 2014 Citations 2014 SCC 71 Prior history APPEAL from Bhasin v. Hrynew , 2013 ABCA 98 (18 March 2013), setting aside Bhasin (Bhasin & Associates) v. Hrynew , 2011 ABQB 637 (4 November 2011). Leave to appeal granted, Harish Bhasin, carrying on business as Bhasin & Associates v. Larry Hrynew, et al. , 2013 CanLII 53400 (22 August 2013), Supreme Court (Canada) Ruling Appeal allowed in part. Holding The trial judge did not make a reversible error by adjudicating the issue of good faith. Her detailed findings amply support the overall conclusion that C acted dishonestly with B throughout the period leading up to its exercise of the non‑renewal clause in question. Court membership Chief Justice: Beverley McLachlin Puisne Justices: Louis LeBel , Rosalie Abella , Marshall Rothstein , Thomas Cromwell , Michael Moldaver , Andromache Karakatsanis , Richard Wagner Reasons given Unanimous reasons by Cromwell J Moldaver J took no part in the consideration or decision of the case. Bhasin v Hrynew , 2014 SCC 71 is a leading Canadian contract law case, concerning good faith as a basic organizing principle in contractual relations in Canada's common law jurisdictions. Facts [ edit ] Canadian American Financial Corp (now known as Heritage Education Funds ) marketed Registered Education Saving Plans , and hired Bhasin as an "enrollment director" in 1998 for three years with automatic renewal unless six months' notice was given. Hrynew, another enrollment director who competed, had proposed to merge their agencies and asked Can-Am to force it, but Bhasin refused. Then, Can-Am appointed Hrynew as a "provincial trading officer" to review compliance with the Alberta Securities Commission 's regulations, which meant auditing enrollment directors, with the power to review confidential business records. Bhasin objected. In June 2000, Can-Am told the Commission it would restruct
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the obligation to return confidential information, moving from 'shall promptly return' to 'may, at their discretion, return some information', and excluding personal notes. This contradicts the principle of good faith in contractual performance. The Law Explanation suggests that this could be seen as violating the duty of good faith, referencing Bhasin v Hrynew, 2014 SCC 71. Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms that Bhasin v Hrynew, 2014 SCC 71 is a leading Canadian contract law case, concerning good faith as a basic organizing principle in contractual relations in Canada's common law jurisdictions. The changes in the contract undermine the purpose of the NDA, thus a legal contradiction exists.
